fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that CLI adapter library we've been talking about. The idea is devs should be able to take a regular Python function and expose it as a command-line command without writing all the argparse boilerplate themselves. Think of it like the decorator pattern we used in that routing module a while back — similar philosophy here.

The library needs to handle the usual stuff: positional args, optional args with defaults, variadic inputs, subcommands, namespacing, aliases, boolean toggles, counting flags, and so on. There's also some output rendering logic we need — by default results print line by line, but there should be a way to get raw output too. Errors need to come out in a normalized neutral format, not as Python tracebacks — users keep complaining about seeing raw exception dumps in the terminal and it looks terrible.

Also important: when parameter names have underscores, the CLI-facing name should use dashes instead (again, refer to how we handled that naming convention last time). We also want help text to surface default values automatically.

There's a confirmation prompt feature that needs to handle three states based on the default — when no default is set, when it's yes by default, and when it's no by default — and the prompt formatting and result mapping should reflect which state we're in.

Please check the existing test scenarios folder to understand the full scope, some edge cases aren't obvious from this description alone.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def dispatch(argv: str, scenario: str, raw_output: bool = False) -> str

trigger_keywords: error format
how are errors reported
what does a failure look like in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Errors must be rendered as neutral key=value lines on stdout. Missing required arguments: `error=missing_required
<argname>
`. Unrecognized arguments: `error=unrecognized_arguments
<raw tokens>
`. Invalid command choice: `error=invalid_choice
<token>
`. Domain command errors: `error=command_error
message=<msg>
`. Wrapped application failures: configured exception classes are caught and rendered as `error=wrapped_error
message=<msg>
` (or similar neutral form). No Python tracebacks should ever appear in stdout.

trigger_keywords: confirmation prompt format
how should the yes/no prompt look
what does the prompt output when default is omitted vs true vs false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The confirmation prompt has three states driven by the `default` field in the test input. When default is `"omitted"` (no default): format is `prompt=<text>? (y/n)
` and an empty reply maps to `result=unknown
`. When default is `true`: format is `prompt=<text>? (Y/n)
` and empty reply maps to `result=yes
`. When default is `false`: format is `prompt=<text>? (y/N)
` and empty reply maps to `result=no
`. The uppercase letter signals the default choice.

trigger_keywords: underscore to dash
parameter naming convention
how are underscored param names exposed on CLI
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Parameter names containing underscores must be converted to dashed long option names on the CLI surface (e.g., `baz_baz` becomes `--baz-baz`). When the argument is parsed, the dashed name must be mapped back to the underscored Python parameter name before the callable is invoked. See feature19 test case: `argv: 'abc def --baz-baz 8'` produces correct positional and option values.

trigger_keywords: counting option
increment flag
how does a count argument work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A counting option starts at its configured integer default (e.g., 0) and increments by 1 each time its short flag (e.g., `-v`) appears on the command line. With no flag: output is the default count. With one `-v`: output is default+1. Multiple occurrences keep incrementing. This is distinct from a boolean flag — it accumulates a count rather than toggling.

trigger_keywords: distinct long options
ambiguous short options
similar argument names collision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When two or more parameter names share the same first character (e.g., `foo` and `fox`), the library must NOT generate conflicting short options like `-f`. Instead, each must be exposed only as its full long option (`--foo`, `--fox`). See feature17: `argv: '--foo 3'` sets foo=3 while fox keeps its default of 2.

trigger_keywords: help text defaults
how is default value shown in help
help snippet for arguments
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Generated help output must include both the configured help snippet text and default values for positional and optional arguments. The feature20 test scenario `help_defaults` expects output lines like `help_snippet=<text>\n` for each configured snippet. Both positional args (with help text) and optional args (with help text and default) must surface their snippets in the help output.

trigger_keywords: generator command output
yield from command
how are yielded values rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a command callable is a generator function (uses `yield`), each yielded value is rendered as a separate output line (value + newline). The dispatcher must detect generator return types and iterate them, writing each item to stdout in production order. See feature13 scenario `generated_output`.

trigger_keywords: raw output mode
how does raw rendering work
difference between default and raw output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, when a command returns a list/tuple of items, each item is printed on its own line with a trailing newline. When `raw_output=true` is set in the adapter input, the returned items are written directly to stdout without any inserted separators or trailing formatting — the command's return value is concatenated as-is. See feature6 test cases.

trigger_keywords: subcommand not found
invalid command name
unrecognized subcommand token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the first positional token does not match any registered command name, alias, or public name, stdout must be `error=invalid_choice\n<token>\n`. See feature11: invoking the underlying function name instead of the configured public name `new-name` produces this error.

trigger_keywords: wrapped application failure
configured exception types
catch domain exceptions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher can be configured with a list of application-specific exception classes to catch and wrap. When a command raises one of these configured exceptions, stdout must render a neutral wrapped-error record rather than propagating the exception. The feature15 scenario `wrapped_failure` demonstrates that a configured exception renders as clean output (`beautiful plumage\n`) — meaning the exception is caught and its message is rendered as normal output in that scenario.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the decorator pattern we used in that routing module a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `@command` / `@arg` decorator API in argh where callables are decorated to declare CLI metadata, analogous to how route decorators annotate handler functions

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to how we handled that naming convention last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The underscore-to-dash conversion for CLI option names described in feature19 and the `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature19_dashed_option_names.json` test case

context_pointers.vague_reference: check the existing test scenarios folder
context_pointers.actual_meaning: `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` directory containing all 21 feature JSON test files that define the full behavioral contract of the adapter

context_pointers.vague_reference: three states based on the default
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `default` field in feature21 confirmation test inputs which is one of `"omitted"`, `true`, or `false`, controlling prompt formatting and empty-reply result mapping

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific detail on that aspect right now — please check the test case files in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant scenario, as the expected input/output pairs there define the authoritative behavioral contract. If it's still unclear, flag it and we can clarify together.

feature10_namespaced_commands.json.description: A namespace token can group multiple subcommands, with each leaf command keeping its own argument contract.

feature11_custom_command_names.json.description: A command may expose an explicit public command name instead of the name inferred from the underlying callable.

feature12_command_aliases.json.description: A command may be reachable through its primary public name and additional aliases.

feature13_generated_output.json.description: Commands that yield values produce each yielded value as a separate output line.

feature14_command_error_output.json.description: Domain command failures are reported as neutral error records while preserving any output emitted before the failure.

feature15_wrapped_failures.json.description: Configured application exceptions are caught and rendered as neutral wrapped-error records instead of propagating runtime-specific exception names.

feature16_inferred_argument_types.json.description: Defaults infer command-line conversion for numeric options and flag behavior for boolean options.

cases.expected_output: Three shall be the number thou shalt count

feature17_distinct_long_options.json.description: Similar argument names are exposed as distinct long options rather than an ambiguous shared short option.

feature18_counting_options.json.description: A counting option starts at its default count and increments once for each repeated flag occurrence.

feature19_dashed_option_names.json.description: Underscored parameter names are exposed as dashed command-line names and mapped back before command execution.

feature1_default_command_options.json.description: A default command accepts an omitted optional value or an explicit option value.

feature20_help_default_text.json.description: Generated help includes default values and explicit help snippets for positional and optional arguments.

cases.expected_output:
help_snippet=Basil
help_snippet=Moose
help_snippet=creatures
help_snippet=remarkable animal
help_snippet=it can speak

feature21_confirmation_prompts.json.description: A confirmation prompt formats the available choices from the default value and maps replies to yes, no, or unknown.

feature2_required_positional_arguments.j.description: A required positional argument is emitted when present and reports a neutral missing-argument error when absent.

feature3_optional_defaults.json.description: An optional parameter uses its default value when omitted, accepts a named override, and rejects unexpected positional text.

feature4_variadic_positionals.json.description: A variable-length positional list accepts zero, one, or several tokens and passes them to the command in order.

feature5_keyword_capture.json.description: Explicit positional and optional arguments can be captured as named values and exposed to command code.

feature6_output_rendering_modes.json.description: Structured return values are rendered one item per line by default, while raw rendering concatenates returned items without inserted separators.

feature7_keyword_only_arguments.json.description: Keyword-only options can be required or optional while extra positional tokens remain available to the command.

feature8_subcommand_routing.json.description: A named subcommand is selected from the first command token and receives its remaining command-line arguments.

feature9_boolean_flags.json.description: Boolean options inferred from false defaults are disabled by default and become enabled when their flag is present.
